title: Chapter 17: The Grose
content: I don't find her in the kitchen.

That's where I've always found her — at the long steel counter, her back to the door, hands in flour or around a knife or wrapped around a mug of something that never stopped steaming. The kitchen was Mrs. Grose's country. Her borders were the pantry door and the breakfast nook and the window over the sink where the light came in yellow and thin. I learned early not to cross those borders without invitation.

But the kitchen is empty. The stove is cold. A single mug sits by the drain, washed and upturned, and the silence in the room has a quality I've only ever noticed in churches — that specific hush of a space where someone has just stopped praying.
I stand there long enough to feel foolish. Then I notice the door.

It's behind the back kitchen, past the scullery, in a corner of Bly I didn't know existed until this moment. A narrow door with a brass handle gone soft with age, the metal worn to a satin finish by thousands of hands. Someone has been opening and closing this door for a very long time.
I turn the handle. The door gives without sound.

The room on the other side is small. Smaller than my bedroom at the east wing, smaller than the pantry, smaller than any room in a house this grand has a right to be. A sitting room, or what was once a sitting room — now it's something between a nest and an archive. Every surface is occupied. The bookshelves are stuffed with volumes whose spines I can't read from the doorway. The mantel is crowded with photographs in frames of every size and metal — silver, brass, one that might be pewter, one that's just bare wood. The walls have more photographs, pinned and taped and resting on small nails, a collage so dense the wallpaper shows through only in thin veins of faded rose.

The smell reaches me before I step inside: lavender and old wood and something underneath both, something warm and close, like linen that's been folded while still damp. A coal grate glows in the corner, and the heat from it presses against my face like a palm.
And there, in the center of all of it, in a chair that has molded itself to her shape over what must be decades, sits Mrs. Grose.

She's mending a shirt. One of Miles's, I think — the collar is frayed, and her hands move with a needle and dark thread in a rhythm so practiced it looks like breathing. She doesn't look up when I enter. She doesn't startle. She doesn't even pause.
She's not surprised.

I stand in the doorway. The warmth seeps through me. The photographs crowd at the edges of my vision — faces, faces, so many faces, and I realize with a small shock that some of them are children. Different children. Children I don't recognize. Children who must have been here before the twins, before me, before any of us.
"Close the door," Mrs. Grose says.
I close it. The latch makes a sound like a whispered word.

She finishes a stitch. Bites the thread. Lays the shirt across her lap and looks at me, and for the first time I see her clearly — not the housekeeper, not the gatekeeper, not the woman who appears with toast and disappears with laundry, but the woman who has lived in this small warm room for thirty years and filled it with the faces of everyone who has passed through Bly and left.
"What happened to Rebecca Jessel?"

I don't mean to ask it like that. I mean to approach it differently — carefully, the way you approach a bird on a windowsill. But the words come out plain and hard, and they land on the floor between us like something dropped.
Mrs. Grose's hands stop.
The silence stretches. The coal shifts in the grate. A thread of smoke rises and vanishes.
Then she says, "She left. In the night. She took nothing. I found her room empty the next morning."
Her voice is different in this room. Flatter. The professional softness is gone. She's not performing housekeeping; she's just speaking.
"She didn't leave," I say. "She wouldn't leave the journal behind."
"Then perhaps she didn't leave by choice."

The words hang. Mrs. Grose holds my gaze. And in her eyes I see something I don't expect — not guilt, not evasion, not the careful blankness she wears in the kitchen. Something older. A sediment. Thirty years of something settling to the bottom of her and compacting into stone.
Resignation.
I step closer. My hand finds the back of a wooden chair, and I grip it. "You know what happened to her."

"I know what happened before her. And before the one before her. And before the one before that." She picks up the needle again, but she doesn't resume stitching. She holds it between her thumb and forefinger, turning it slightly, watching the firelight travel along its length. "I have been at Bly for thirty years, Maeve. I was here before your father came. I was here when he died. I was here for every—" She pauses. The needle stills. "For every one."
"Every governess."

"Every governess. Every tutor. Every companion. Every bright, capable woman who walked through that front door thinking she understood what she was walking into." She looks at me, and something in her expression shifts — not pity, exactly, but adjacent to it. "You all think you understand. You all think you're the one who'll figure it out. And every time, I watch. And every time, I keep the house."
The photographs on the wall press against my awareness. All those faces. All those women.
"How many?" I ask.

"Enough that I've stopped counting. Enough that I've learned the shape of it." She sets the needle down. Her hands rest on the shirt in her lap, still. "I keep the house. That's all I've ever done. I keep the house and I keep the children and I keep the secrets. It's the same job."
"It's not the same job."

"It is here." She says it without emphasis. Without drama. Just a plain statement of fact, the way someone might say the roof leaks or the garden faces east. "At Bly, keeping the house means keeping the secrets. The two things can't be separated. I learned that in the first year."

I think about the kitchen. About all the meals she's made, all the linens she's folded, all the mornings she's stood at that counter with her back to the door. I think about how thoroughly she made herself into furniture. How deliberately invisible she became.
"Did my father know?" The question comes out quieter than I intend. "Did he know what this house does to people?"
Something moves across her face. A flicker — not anger, not quite. Something with teeth in it. "Your father built the control room. Your father designed the Game."
"Someone changed it after he died. The rules are different now — the twins said—"

"The twins changed the surface. The structure is still Peter's." She shakes her head, once. A small, tired motion. "He was a brilliant man. I won't deny that. And he was a cruel one. I won't deny that either. The two things lived side by side in him, and neither one erased the other."

I want to argue. I want to defend him, or condemn him, or do something other than stand here absorbing the weight of this woman's certainty. But I don't know enough. I've never known enough. I came to Bly with a dead man's name and a handful of questions, and every answer has opened ten more.
"Mrs. Grose," I say, and I'm surprised by how steady my voice is, "I need to know what's on those computers. The control room. The files. All of it."

She looks at me for a long time. The fire crackles. The lavender smell is thick now, almost soporific, and I'm aware of the warmth pressing against me like something alive, like the room itself is holding me.
Then Mrs. Grose stands.

She's shorter than I realize — or maybe I've never seen her standing still, without the context of the kitchen to give her scale. She moves to the corner of the room, to a cabinet I hadn't noticed, built into the wall behind a curtain of faded fabric. She draws the curtain aside. Opens the cabinet.

Inside, there's a box. Tin, by the look of it, old and slightly rusted at the edges. She takes it down and opens it, and from inside she withdraws something that catches the firelight and holds it — a small black USB drive, plain and unmarked, its plastic casing reflecting the glow like a dark eye.
"Peter's files," she says. "The ones from the control room. I made a copy. Before."
"Before what?"
"Before they changed the locks. Before Henry sent the technician. Before—" She stops. Swallows. "Before."
She holds it out to me. The USB drive sits in her palm, small and black and ordinary. It could be anyone's. It could hold anything.
"You can take this," she says. "But if you do, you can't go back. You'll know everything. And everything is heavier than you think."

I look at the drive. I look at her face. The resignation is still there, but there's something else underneath it now — something that might be hope, or might be exhaustion. I can't tell the difference.
"Has anyone else taken it?"
"Not from me."
"But others have known? About the files?"
"Others have known." She doesn't elaborate. She doesn't have to. The photographs on the wall are loud enough.

I reach out. My fingers close around the USB drive. The plastic is warm from the cabinet, or from her hand, or from the fire that's been burning in this room for thirty years. It weighs almost nothing. It weighs everything.
Mrs. Grose nods.

It's not approval. It's not disapproval. It's something else entirely — something I've only seen once before, in the eyes of a woman at my mother's funeral who looked at me and said, "You have her mouth." Recognition. Completion. Like I've just walked into a room Mrs. Grose has been keeping ready for decades.
"Your mother was the best person I've ever known," she says. She sits back down. Picks up the needle. The shirt lands across her lap again. "Peter didn't deserve her."
I don't say anything. I can't. My throat has closed around something sharp and unnameable, and I know that if I try to speak I'll make a sound I won't be able to take back.
"And you," Mrs. Grose continues, threading the needle with a motion so smooth it looks like conjuring, "you have her kindness and his cunning. That's a dangerous combination."

She doesn't look at me when she says it. Her hands resume their rhythm — in, through, out, pull. The mending continues. The world hasn't changed. The fire still burns. The lavender still fills the room with its old, sweet, suffocating comfort. But everything is different.

I stand there. The USB drive sits in my closed fist, its plastic edge pressing a line into my palm. The warmth of the room is almost too much now, or maybe it's the right amount and I'm the one who's too much — too alert, too aware, too full of the knowledge that every woman who stood where I'm standing made the same choice. Took the same risk. Believed she was the one who could carry it.
None of them are here to tell me how it ended.

I look at the photographs again. The faces look back at me — some smiling, some not, some caught mid-laugh, some staring into the camera with expressions I recognize because I've worn them myself. Hope. Determination. The particular bravery of someone who still believes the story she's telling herself.
Mrs. Grose stitches in silence.
I turn to go. My hand finds the door handle — the same brass, the same softness, worn down by all those hands before mine. I pause.
"You were never on their side," I say. Not a question.
"No."
"On Henry's side?"
"No."
"My father's?"
The needle pauses. Just for a moment. Then it resumes. "Not his either."
"Whose side, then?"

She doesn't look up. One more stitch. Another. The firelight moves across her face, and in its shifting I see it — the truth I've been circling since I walked through that door, maybe since I walked through Bly's front door twenty-five days ago.
"The house's," she says.

And the way she says it — quiet, certain, unashamed — I understand that she doesn't mean the building. She doesn't mean the walls and the roof and the pipes and the wires. She means something older than architecture. Something that was here before the house was built, that the house was built around, that has been waiting in the ground of this place since before anyone thought to put a name on it.
She means Bly. Not the manor. The thing underneath.
And the house — the house wants the Game to end.

I open the door. I step through. I close it behind me, and the latch makes that same sound, that small whispered word, and I stand in the cold corridor with the USB drive clenched in my fist and the echo of lavender still in my nose and I think:
*But what does it mean for the house to want something?*
And worse:
*What does it mean that I'm the one it's chosen to want it through?*

The corridor is dark. The kitchen is empty. Somewhere above me, the twins are sleeping, or scheming, or simply waiting for the next round. Somewhere out there, Henry is counting his money and Peter is dead and Rebecca Jessel is gone, gone in the night, without her journal, without her clothes, without a single trace to mark her passage through these halls.
And here, in the dark, I hold the tiny black weight of everything in my hand.
I go upstairs. I don't look back.

But I feel it — the presence of that small room behind me, the warmth of it, the woman inside it still stitching, still mending, still keeping the house. A woman who isn't good. Who isn't bad. Who is something else entirely, something that thirty years of compromise have forged into a shape that no word in our language quite fits.
Mrs. Grose. The Grose.
The keeper of the house.
I climb the stairs. My hand aches where the USB drive's edge has pressed its line into my palm. I don't loosen my grip.
The house wants the Game to end.
I think I do too.
